repository: Lewis University Library AI Vibe Code Repository
description: Prototype application dataset for the AI Vibe Code Repository. Each project record represents a browser-based, AI-assisted interactive application built by Lewis faculty and staff through human-directed coding. Authors are placeholder labels until contributor profiles are confirmed.

projects.title: Assignment AI Fit Checker
projects.description: Walks an instructor through four questions about a planned assignment — what students should actually learn, what evidence of their process matters, what the integrity stakes are, and how transparent the AI rules need to be — and lands on one of four stances: prohibit, permit, scaffold, or require. Generates draft syllabus language faculty can edit and adapt.
projects.author: Faculty Contributor (Pending)
projects.purpose: Replace 'should I allow AI on this assignment?' uncertainty with a structured conversation. The recommendation is a starting point for faculty judgment, not a verdict.
projects.ai_role: Claude drafted the branching logic and sample syllabus passages from a faculty-written rubric. The contributor rewrote roughly half of the recommendations to remove compliance-heavy framing and to honor disciplinary differences.
projects.human_review: Tested against three sample syllabi from English, Nursing, and Aviation. Tone was tuned toward collegial conversation rather than policy enforcement.
projects.accessibility_notes: Keyboard-accessible form controls, high-contrast result panels, aria-live recommendation updates, printable summary.

projects.title: Research Source Triage Desk
projects.description: A first-pass source evaluation guide. Students paste a URL or citation and answer short prompts about authorship, transparency, and corroboration; the app responds with a confidence read and points to the next move — keep it, verify laterally, or set it aside.
projects.author: Faculty Contributor (Pending)
projects.purpose: Give students a structured habit for the first sixty seconds with any source, before the deeper evaluation work begins.
projects.ai_role: ChatGPT generated the prompt scaffolding and example feedback strings. The librarian-contributor rewrote the lateral-reading guidance to align with the SIFT method and removed several confidently wrong examples about identifying peer-reviewed work.
projects.human_review: Reviewed against current information-literacy frameworks; sample feedback was pilot-tested with two sections of INFO 100.
projects.accessibility_notes: Plain-language prompts at a 9th-grade reading level, screen-reader-tested result panels, high-contrast confidence indicators.

projects.title: Citation Quest
projects.description: A practice game where students identify missing metadata, choose between citation styles, and rebuild broken references. Each round shows what makes a citation actually useful — finding the source again — rather than treating it as punctuation drills.
projects.author: Faculty Contributor (Pending)
projects.purpose: Reframe citation from compliance task to research skill. Students who can spot what's missing from a reference understand what citations are for.
projects.ai_role: Claude generated the puzzle bank and scoring logic. The contributor rejected about a third of the puzzles for being trivial or stylistically inconsistent and added edge cases drawn from real reference desk questions.
projects.human_review: Playtested with first-year students; question difficulty curve was adjusted twice based on observed frustration points.
projects.accessibility_notes: Full keyboard navigation, no time-pressure mode, dyslexia-friendly font option, descriptive alt text on all icons.

projects.title: AI Disclosure Builder
projects.description: A drafting tool for clear AI-use disclosures on coursework, presentations, and research. Users describe what the AI did, what they did, and what the assignment required; the app produces a short paragraph in plain language — ready to paste, edit, or use as a model.
projects.author: Faculty Contributor (Pending)
projects.purpose: Make honest disclosure easy enough that students and staff actually do it, instead of guessing at what to say.
projects.ai_role: Claude proposed phrasing patterns for common disclosure scenarios. The librarian-contributor rewrote the output templates to remove hedging language and to make the writer's voice (not the AI's role) the grammatical subject.
projects.human_review: Sample outputs reviewed by two faculty integrity officers; phrasing now reflects current institutional disclosure expectations.
projects.accessibility_notes: Single-column form, copy-to-clipboard with confirmation, plain-text export, no required fields beyond the writer's intent.

projects.title: Library Impact Dashboard
projects.description: Turns library activity data — instruction sessions, consultations, gate counts, database use — into short readable stories rather than charts that need a footnote. Each panel pairs a number with a sentence about what it means and a year-over-year comparison.
projects.author: Faculty Contributor (Pending)
projects.purpose: Help annual reports and stakeholder conversations start from meaning, not from a CSV.
projects.ai_role: Claude drafted the narrative sentence templates that surround each metric. The contributor rejected several phrasings that overstated impact and added hedging language where the data warranted caution.
projects.human_review: Reviewed by the assessment librarian; numbers and narrative are kept in sync via a single source CSV that contributors can swap.
projects.accessibility_notes: Color-blind-safe palette, full data table fallback for every chart, sortable and downloadable raw data.

projects.title: Aviation Fuel Planning Calculator
projects.description: A training calculator for estimating fuel under changing route, reserve, alternate, and weather assumptions. Students change one variable at a time and watch the safety margins shift — the point is to build intuition for which factors actually move the needle.
projects.author: Faculty Contributor (Pending)
projects.purpose: Turn fuel planning from a formula to memorize into a sensitivity analysis students can feel.
projects.ai_role: Claude generated the underlying calculation scaffolding from FAA reference values supplied by the contributor. The instructor verified all reserve and alternate logic against current Part 91 standards and rewrote three calculation paths that had been simplified incorrectly.
projects.human_review: Validated against worked examples from AVIA 220; outputs cross-checked with a flight instructor before publication.
projects.accessibility_notes: Numerical inputs with unit toggles, clear validation messages, results tables paired with plain-language summaries, printable flight-plan-style output.

projects.title: Weather Decision Trainer
projects.description: Presents a developing weather scenario — METARs, TAFs, PIREPs, freezing levels — and asks the student to make a sequence of go/no-go calls. After each decision, the app reveals what an experienced pilot weighed and why, including the close calls that don't have a single right answer.
projects.author: Faculty Contributor (Pending)
projects.purpose: Practice the kind of judgment that a checkride examines but a textbook can't teach: living with uncertain forecasts.
projects.ai_role: ChatGPT helped generate scenario variations and distractor decision paths from a small set of seed cases. The instructor rejected scenarios that were too clean (real weather is rarely a clear go or no-go) and added three based on actual incidents.
projects.human_review: Each scenario reviewed by a CFI; rationales were rewritten to model professional reasoning rather than rule citation.
projects.accessibility_notes: Text-equivalent for every weather chart, screen-reader-friendly METAR breakdowns, no time pressure on decisions.

projects.title: Weight & Balance Sandbox
projects.description: A draggable loading sandbox: students place passengers, fuel, and baggage in a generic light aircraft and watch the center of gravity move in real time. The envelope turns red before flight is unsafe, not at the moment of failure, so students learn to plan with margin.
projects.author: Faculty Contributor (Pending)
projects.purpose: Build a felt sense of how loading decisions accumulate. Numbers tell you the answer; the visualization tells you the reason.
projects.ai_role: Claude generated the SVG envelope rendering and the moment-arm calculations from a set of fictional aircraft profiles supplied by the instructor. The contributor verified all envelope shapes and replaced one profile that was unrealistic.
projects.human_review: Cross-checked against worked examples; envelope and station data are stored separately so other instructors can swap aircraft.
projects.accessibility_notes: Drag-and-drop with full keyboard alternatives, numerical entry mode, high-contrast envelope display.

projects.title: Airspace Quizzer
projects.description: A rapid-fire airspace identification game. Students see a sectional chart excerpt, identify the airspace class, and answer a short follow-up about the requirements — equipment, communication, weather minimums. The app tracks which categories trip the student up and serves more of those.
projects.author: Faculty Contributor (Pending)
projects.purpose: Convert a memorization-heavy topic into a habit-forming pattern-recognition exercise.
projects.ai_role: Claude generated the question bank from a set of seed sectional excerpts. The instructor flagged about a quarter of the questions as ambiguous or out-of-date and rewrote them.
projects.human_review: Reviewed against current FAR/AIM references; question bank is dated and versioned for periodic review.
projects.accessibility_notes: Full keyboard control, descriptive text alternatives for chart excerpts, untimed practice mode alongside the timed mode.

projects.title: CRM Scenario Replays
projects.description: Short replays of cockpit and cabin communication scenarios drawn from the human-factors literature. Students mark where the breakdown began and propose a different intervention; the app compares their answer to documented analyses and shows where reasonable people disagree.
projects.author: Faculty Contributor (Pending)
projects.purpose: Train the noticing — the moment to speak up — rather than the script.
projects.ai_role: ChatGPT drafted scenario dialog from incident summaries supplied by the instructor. The contributor rewrote dialog that read too theatrically and removed cases that telegraphed the answer.
projects.human_review: Each scenario references its source incident; analytical commentary was reviewed for fidelity to current CRM teaching.
projects.accessibility_notes: Transcripts for all dialog, no audio-only content, high-contrast timeline markers.

projects.title: Clinical Handoff Simulator
projects.description: Browser-based practice for SBAR-style patient handoffs. Students receive a brief patient chart, draft a handoff in stages, and get structured feedback against a rubric — including the parts a checklist can miss, like prioritization and acknowledging what they don't yet know.
projects.author: Faculty Contributor (Pending)
projects.purpose: Give students reps before clinicals where the cost of a fumbled handoff is higher.
projects.ai_role: Claude generated patient scenarios and rubric feedback strings from clinical exemplars supplied by the contributor. About a fifth of the generated charts were rejected for clinical inaccuracy or unrealistic detail; the rest were edited for tone.
projects.human_review: Reviewed by clinical faculty; scenarios were calibrated to match the complexity students encounter in second-semester clinicals.
projects.accessibility_notes: Plain-language scenario summaries, transcript-only mode (no audio dependency), aria-live feedback on rubric scores.

projects.title: Medication Reconciliation Trainer
projects.description: Students compare a patient's home medication list with admission orders, flag discrepancies, and decide which warrant clarification, which warrant a hold, and which are nothing. Each case ends with a debrief that distinguishes documentation issues from clinical risk.
projects.author: Faculty Contributor (Pending)
projects.purpose: Practice the noticing that prevents quiet errors. Reconciliation is mostly a reading skill, so this app is mostly a reading exercise.
projects.ai_role: Claude drafted patient medication histories from a set of seed cases. The nursing-faculty contributor verified every drug interaction flag and rewrote several debriefs that confused therapeutic duplication with allergy.
projects.human_review: Reviewed by a clinical pharmacist; case complexity scaled across three difficulty tiers.
projects.accessibility_notes: Clear typography for medication names, generic/brand toggle, sortable comparison tables, screen-reader-tested.

projects.title: Pediatric Vitals Pattern Reader
projects.description: A visualization-led practice tool that shows pediatric vital signs over time and asks students to read the trend before the alarm fires. Age-appropriate normal ranges shift as the patient's profile changes; the goal is to recognize compensatory patterns before they decompensate.
projects.author: Faculty Contributor (Pending)
projects.purpose: Teach trend reading. Single-point vitals interpretation is straightforward; reading the slope is what experienced pediatric nurses actually do.
projects.ai_role: Claude generated vitals time-series data within faculty-supplied parameters. The contributor rejected several physiologically implausible curves and added three based on real (de-identified) cases.
projects.human_review: Reviewed by pediatric clinical faculty; reference ranges sourced and dated for periodic update.
projects.accessibility_notes: Color-coded zones with text labels, sonification option for trend direction, full data tables alongside every chart.

projects.title: End-of-Life Conversation Rehearsal
projects.description: A text-based rehearsal of difficult conversations: prognosis disclosure, goals-of-care, family disagreement at the bedside. The app does not score 'right answers' — it surfaces phrasings that close the conversation versus phrasings that keep it open, with attention to silence and to what the family has not yet asked.
projects.author: Faculty Contributor (Pending)
projects.purpose: Make space for the part of nursing that words can carry but rubrics rarely capture.
projects.ai_role: Claude drafted dialog scaffolding from palliative-care literature supplied by the contributor. The faculty member rewrote roughly two-thirds of the generated lines, which were too procedural; the final dialog was workshopped with a chaplain and a hospice nurse.
projects.human_review: Reviewed for cultural and religious sensitivity; alternate phrasings included to avoid prescribing a single conversational style.
projects.accessibility_notes: Pause-friendly pacing, no time pressure, content-warning landing page, exit-anytime button visible throughout.

projects.title: IV Drip Rate Bench
projects.description: A drip rate practice bench. Students enter the order, fluid, and tubing, then calculate the rate; the app checks the answer, shows the work, and asks a follow-up question — what if the patient's status changes mid-infusion?
projects.author: Faculty Contributor (Pending)
projects.purpose: Build calculation fluency that survives stress. The follow-up questions are where the learning is.
projects.ai_role: Claude generated the problem bank from faculty-supplied templates. The contributor verified every answer and tightened the follow-up prompts so they tested clinical reasoning rather than arithmetic.
projects.human_review: Reviewed against current dosage-calculation textbooks; answer keys checked twice independently.
projects.accessibility_notes: Clear labeling of units, error tolerance with explanation, screen-reader-friendly equation walkthroughs.

projects.title: Lesson Activity Generator Studio
projects.description: An active-learning activity drafter. Instructors enter a learning outcome, class size, room layout, and time block; the app proposes three activity sketches with timing, materials, and a quick check for understanding. Each sketch is editable inline.
projects.author: Faculty Contributor (Pending)
projects.purpose: Move past the blank-page problem in lesson planning. The first draft is cheap; the editing is where the teaching happens.
projects.ai_role: Claude generated activity templates from a faculty-curated bank of evidence-based active-learning structures. The contributor rejected templates that drifted from the underlying pedagogy and added discipline-specific examples.
projects.human_review: Reviewed against active-learning literature; activity bank is tagged by underlying technique so substitutions are transparent.
projects.accessibility_notes: Outcome-driven structure works at any grade level, plain-language outputs, exportable as printable lesson cards.

projects.title: Rubric Feedback Calibrator
projects.description: A practice space for tuning rubric feedback before it lands on students. Faculty paste in a draft comment, mark the rubric criterion, and the app surfaces tone, specificity, and actionability — flagging when feedback is correct but unusable.
projects.author: Faculty Contributor (Pending)
projects.purpose: The hardest part of giving feedback isn't accuracy — it's whether the student can do anything with it. This app is about that gap.
projects.ai_role: Claude scored sample feedback against a rubric of feedback quality dimensions. The contributor flagged several places where the AI rewarded performative warmth over substance and rebalanced the scoring.
projects.human_review: Reviewed by faculty-development staff; calibrated against feedback samples from three departments.
projects.accessibility_notes: Single-textarea entry, clear category breakdown, copy-back-to-LMS friendly format.

projects.title: Differentiation Mover
projects.description: A planning aid for adapting a single lesson across content, process, product, and learning environment. Pre-service teachers describe their lesson and one challenge in their classroom; the app suggests two specific adjustments with rationale grounded in UDL principles.
projects.author: Faculty Contributor (Pending)
projects.purpose: Help new teachers move from 'I should differentiate' to a concrete first move.
projects.ai_role: ChatGPT generated suggestion templates anchored in UDL guidelines supplied by the contributor. About a quarter of the suggestions were rejected as superficial; the rest were edited to attach to specific student needs rather than generic categories.
projects.human_review: Reviewed against current UDL framework; suggestions tagged by guideline so users can see the reasoning.
projects.accessibility_notes: The tool itself follows UDL principles — multiple input modes, plain-language output, expandable rationale panels.

projects.title: Parent Conference Rehearsal
projects.description: A text-based rehearsal of parent-teacher conversations across registers — the supportive parent, the skeptical parent, the parent who is scared. Pre-service teachers practice opening a difficult conversation, holding the structure, and naming what the student did well alongside what needs work.
projects.author: Faculty Contributor (Pending)
projects.purpose: Field experience offers limited reps for difficult family conversations. This is a low-stakes place to make the awkward first attempts.
projects.ai_role: Claude drafted parent voices from scenario sketches supplied by the contributor. The faculty member rewrote dialog that flattened parents into types and removed two scenarios that played on stereotypes.
projects.human_review: Reviewed by a school-community liaison; scenarios deliberately include moments where the teacher's first read was wrong.
projects.accessibility_notes: Text-only; pause and revise turns; exit-conversation option always visible.

projects.title: Poetry Linebreak Studio
projects.description: A drafting space for line breaks and stanza shape. Writers paste prose or unbroken verse, then experiment with break points, indentation, and white space; the app shows how each version reads aloud (estimated cadence) and how the visual weight shifts on the page.
projects.author: Faculty Contributor (Pending)
projects.purpose: Lineation is the part of poetry that beginners feel last. This is a place to feel it earlier.
projects.ai_role: Claude proposed alternate line breaks from a draft and offered short notes on what each version emphasized. The contributor (a poet on faculty) rewrote the explanatory notes substantially, removing readings that were technically right but tonally off.
projects.human_review: Workshopped in two creative-writing sections; the AI's suggestions are presented as one set of options, not as authoritative.
projects.accessibility_notes: Large editable canvas, keyboard navigation between break-points, screen-reader announces structural changes.

projects.title: Argument Map Analyzer
projects.description: Decomposes an essay or op-ed into claims, reasons, evidence, and acknowledgments. Students can map their own draft and see which claims are doing real work and which are filling space; the app deliberately makes weak structural patterns visible without suggesting fixes.
projects.author: Faculty Contributor (Pending)
projects.purpose: Surface structure before suggesting changes. Most struggling drafts aren't poorly worded — they're poorly load-bearing.
projects.ai_role: Claude proposed claim/reason segmentations from pasted text. The faculty contributor noted that the AI tended to over-segment; the final version requires students to confirm or reject each segmentation, which turns out to be the actual learning.
projects.human_review: Reviewed by composition faculty; the friction in the AI's segmentation was preserved as a feature.
projects.accessibility_notes: The visual map is paired with an outline view; both can drive the analysis.

projects.title: Character Voice Distiller
projects.description: Writers paste passages from two characters in their work-in-progress. The app reflects back patterns — sentence-length range, vocabulary register, common syntactic moves — and asks the writer to mark which patterns are intentional and which are bleed-through from the writer's own voice.
projects.author: Faculty Contributor (Pending)
projects.purpose: The most common workshop note is 'all your characters sound the same.' This tool lets the writer see it before someone else has to say it.
projects.ai_role: Claude generated the pattern analysis. The contributor (a fiction writer on faculty) was emphatic that the tool not produce a 'voice score' — the version that ships only describes patterns and asks questions.
projects.human_review: Workshopped in fiction sections; output deliberately stops short of evaluation.
projects.accessibility_notes: Plain-language pattern descriptions, no jargon, side-by-side passage comparison view.

projects.title: Thesis Statement Stress Test
projects.description: Students paste a draft thesis and answer four short questions: What does this claim? Who would disagree? What would change your mind? What's the smallest version of this argument? The app reflects the answers back as a tightened thesis the student can compare to their original.
projects.author: Faculty Contributor (Pending)
projects.purpose: Most weak theses fail one of these four questions. Diagnosing which one is more useful than rewriting blind.
projects.ai_role: Claude generated the reflection logic from a pedagogy bank supplied by composition faculty. The contributor rewrote about half the prompts to be answerable by first-year students; some original phrasings assumed prior coursework students didn't have.
projects.human_review: Pilot-tested in two ENG 110 sections; question phrasing iterated based on observed misreadings.
projects.accessibility_notes: Short prompts, large entry fields, no required answers, exportable side-by-side comparison.

projects.title: Business Crisis Comms Simulator
projects.description: A developing PR crisis unfolds across stakeholder messages, news clips, and social posts. Students draft responses under time pressure; the app reflects how each choice changes downstream coverage. There is no clean win condition — only better and worse trajectories.
projects.author: Faculty Contributor (Pending)
projects.purpose: Practice making decisions when information is incomplete and the next message is already due.
projects.ai_role: Claude generated stakeholder messages and downstream press coverage from a small set of seed scenarios. The contributor rejected scenarios that telegraphed a moral and rewrote several stakeholder voices that all sounded like the same person.
projects.human_review: Reviewed by communication faculty; scenarios deliberately include cases where doing the right thing still produces bad coverage.
projects.accessibility_notes: Pause-anytime, text-only feed, large-print mode, exit-and-resume support.

projects.title: Negotiation Rehearsal Room
projects.description: Text-based negotiation practice across types — distributive, integrative, multi-party. Students take turns with an AI counterparty whose interests and constraints are partially hidden; afterwards, the app reveals what the counterparty was actually optimizing for.
projects.author: Faculty Contributor (Pending)
projects.purpose: Negotiation training tends to assume you know what the other side wants. This app practices the part where you don't.
projects.ai_role: Claude plays the counterparty using rule-based interest profiles supplied by the contributor. The faculty member tightened the profiles after early tests showed the AI was too willing to concede.
projects.human_review: Reviewed by management faculty; counterparty behavior is logged and inspectable so students can debrief honestly.
projects.accessibility_notes: Text-only, pause-friendly, no surprise time pressure beyond what each scenario explicitly states.

projects.title: Market Segment Explorer
projects.description: A draggable segmentation sandbox using a synthetic dataset. Students propose a segmentation logic; the app visualizes how the segments hold together — coherence, distinctness, actionability — and asks what would change about the marketing plan if each segment were the priority.
projects.author: Faculty Contributor (Pending)
projects.purpose: Segmentation is taught as a method but lived as a judgment call. This app shifts the work toward the judgment.
projects.ai_role: Claude generated the synthetic dataset and visualization logic. The contributor verified that the synthetic data behaved like real consumer data in the relevant dimensions and not in misleading ones.
projects.human_review: Reviewed by marketing faculty; dataset documentation makes its synthetic nature explicit.
projects.accessibility_notes: Color-blind-safe palettes, full data tables behind every visualization, keyboard alternative to drag-and-drop.

projects.title: Pitch Deck Self-Review
projects.description: Students paste the text content of each slide; the app asks one diagnostic question per slide ('What does an investor actually take away from this slide?') and surfaces patterns — buried claims, redundant slides, unsupported numbers. It does not rewrite the deck.
projects.author: Faculty Contributor (Pending)
projects.purpose: Most pitch problems are visible on a re-read but invisible during the build. This is a structured re-read.
projects.ai_role: Claude generated the diagnostic question set from a faculty-supplied investor-readiness rubric. The contributor rewrote questions that read as prescriptive, since the goal was reflection rather than direction.
projects.human_review: Reviewed by entrepreneurship faculty; output deliberately stops at diagnosis.
projects.accessibility_notes: Slide-by-slide structure, single-question-at-a-time mode, exportable as a structured self-review document.

projects.title: Lasallian Mission Map
projects.description: An interactive map connecting programs, projects, and campus initiatives to the five Lasallian core principles — faith, service, community, respect, quality education. Users can click a principle to see where it currently lives in practice across campus, and click a program to see which principles it embodies.
projects.author: Faculty Contributor (Pending)
projects.purpose: Make the Lasallian mission tangible rather than aspirational by surfacing where it already lives in everyday work.
projects.ai_role: Claude drafted connection text linking each program to relevant principles. The contributor rewrote about a third of the linkages that read as forced or generic, and removed the AI's tendency to attach 'community' to every program.
projects.human_review: Reviewed by University Mission staff; principle-program connections are sourced and editable.
projects.accessibility_notes: Color-blind-safe principle palette, keyboard-navigable nodes, full table fallback for every map view.

projects.title: Scriptural Reading Compass
projects.description: Helps students name the interpretive lens they're using when they read a passage — historical-critical, literary, theological, devotional, comparative — and shows how the same passage opens differently under each. Students answer short prompts about the questions they're bringing; the app reflects which lens those questions imply.
projects.author: Faculty Contributor (Pending)
projects.purpose: Hermeneutical self-awareness is hard to teach in the abstract. This app makes the lens visible by examining the questions a student actually asks.
projects.ai_role: Claude generated lens descriptions and example readings from primary sources supplied by the contributor. The faculty member rewrote several descriptions that conflated lenses, and tightened the framing of devotional reading so it sat alongside academic methods rather than below them.
projects.human_review: Reviewed by theology faculty; primary-source examples are cited; multiple traditions represented.
projects.accessibility_notes: Plain-language lens descriptions, side-by-side passage comparison, no required reading order.

projects.title: Ethics Case Walkthrough
projects.description: Walks students through ethical cases drawn from professional and everyday life using four traditional approaches — virtue, consequentialist, deontological, and care ethics. Students articulate their first read, then see how each approach reframes the case; the app surfaces where the approaches converge and where they genuinely disagree.
projects.author: Faculty Contributor (Pending)
projects.purpose: Practice making ethical reasoning visible. The disagreements between traditions are pedagogically more valuable than the agreements.
projects.ai_role: Claude drafted case framings and approach summaries from faculty source material. The contributor rewrote several summaries that read as philosophical caricatures and added cases where the four approaches converge on different actions for the same reasons.
projects.human_review: Reviewed by ethics faculty; case sources cited; suitable for use in mixed-tradition discussion sections.
projects.accessibility_notes: Text-only, pause-and-resume, no scoring of student responses, exportable case journal.

projects.title: Liturgical Calendar Companion
projects.description: A browsable companion to the liturgical calendar — readings, themes, traditions, and prayers — designed for students who didn't grow up with it but want to understand what's happening when. Each entry includes a brief plain-language explanation of why this day matters and what the readings ask the community to attend to.
projects.author: Faculty Contributor (Pending)
projects.purpose: Make liturgical literacy hospitable to students approaching it for the first time, without watering down the depth of the tradition.
projects.ai_role: Claude generated brief introductions for each season and feast from contributor-supplied liturgical resources. The campus-ministry contributor rewrote roughly a third of the introductions to remove insider-language and to honor diverse practice within the tradition.
projects.human_review: Reviewed by Campus Ministry; readings sourced from current lectionary; traditions noted with their cultural origins where applicable.
projects.accessibility_notes: Plain-language by default with optional traditional terminology, search by date or theme, screen-reader-tested calendar grid.

projects.title: Data Privacy Escape Room
projects.description: A puzzle-driven game where students unlock the next room by solving privacy-and-data-handling problems — spotting a credential leak in a log file, classifying data sensitivity, choosing the right disclosure under FERPA. The puzzles get harder as the institutional context gets messier.
projects.author: Faculty Contributor (Pending)
projects.purpose: Make privacy reasoning feel like investigation rather than compliance training.
projects.ai_role: Claude generated puzzle scenarios and red-herring details from cybersecurity case material supplied by the contributor. The instructor rewrote scenarios that revealed their answer too early and added two based on real institutional incidents.
projects.human_review: Reviewed by IT and cybersecurity faculty; scenarios reference current FERPA and institutional policy; difficulty curve playtested.
projects.accessibility_notes: Full keyboard control, untimed mode, transcripts of all puzzle dialog, descriptive hints available.

projects.title: Algorithm Tracer
projects.description: Step-by-step visualization of common algorithms — sorts, traversals, dynamic programming — with the option to predict the next step before it happens. Wrong predictions don't punish; they trigger a brief pop-up explaining what the student likely got wrong about the algorithm's invariant.
projects.author: Faculty Contributor (Pending)
projects.purpose: Tracing is the skill that separates students who can read code from students who can write it. This app gives them low-stakes reps.
projects.ai_role: Claude generated the trace animations and the misconception-detection logic. The contributor rewrote several explanation panels that were technically correct but pedagogically backwards — they explained the algorithm before naming what the student misunderstood.
projects.human_review: Reviewed by CS faculty; trace fidelity verified against textbook examples; misconception bank tagged by source.
projects.accessibility_notes: All animations have step-by-step text equivalents, keyboard-controlled stepping, screen-reader-friendly state announcements.

projects.title: API Design Mirror
projects.description: Students paste a draft API specification; the app reflects back the implicit assumptions a future caller would have to make and asks whether each assumption is intended. The point isn't to validate the spec; it's to surface what the spec is silently asking the next developer to know.
projects.author: Faculty Contributor (Pending)
projects.purpose: API design is mostly about what you don't write down. This app makes the unwritten visible.
projects.ai_role: Claude generated the assumption-extraction logic from a faculty-curated bank of API design heuristics. The contributor flagged several places where the AI generated plausible-sounding but generic assumptions and tightened the prompts to surface specification-grounded ones.
projects.human_review: Reviewed by software engineering faculty; output frames assumptions as questions rather than verdicts.
projects.accessibility_notes: Text-only, no automatic rewrites, exportable assumption list as a design-review document.

projects.title: ML Fairness Fitting Room
projects.description: Students adjust thresholds, sampling strategies, and feature selections on a synthetic loan-decision model; the app visualizes how each change shifts outcomes across groups. The dashboard makes it impossible to optimize for all fairness definitions at once — that's the lesson.
projects.author: Faculty Contributor (Pending)
projects.purpose: Fairness in ML is a set of trade-offs, not a check-box. This app makes the trade-offs visible without resolving them on the student's behalf.
projects.ai_role: Claude generated the synthetic dataset and the interactive controls. The contributor verified that the dataset preserved the kinds of disparities students should reckon with, and rewrote the result panels to avoid suggesting that any single fairness criterion was 'the right one.'
projects.human_review: Reviewed by ML and ethics faculty; dataset documentation explicitly notes its synthetic origins and intended pedagogical limits.
projects.accessibility_notes: Color-blind-safe disparity charts, full data tables, plain-language summaries of every fairness metric.

projects.title: Chemistry Buffer Lab Bench
projects.description: An interactive buffer system. Students adjust acid, conjugate base, and concentrations and watch the pH curve respond; the panel asks predictive questions before each adjustment so students commit to a hypothesis before the visualization confirms or contradicts it.
projects.author: Faculty Contributor (Pending)
projects.purpose: Buffer behavior is conceptually simple but feels mysterious to students because the math hides what's moving. This app lets the chemistry move first.
projects.ai_role: Claude generated the curve calculations and predictive prompt set. The contributor verified the chemistry of every edge case (especially around pKa boundaries) and rewrote prompts that gave away the predicted answer in their phrasing.
projects.human_review: Validated against worked examples from CHEM 220; calculation logic separated from UI so other instructors can extend.
projects.accessibility_notes: Color-blind-safe pH scale, sonification option for pH change direction, full data tables, keyboard controls.

projects.title: Reaction Mechanism Walker
projects.description: Step-through visualizations of common organic reaction mechanisms with electron-pushing arrows. Students can pause at any step, predict the next intermediate, and see whether their proposed arrow set leads where they think it does. Wrong arrows produce structurally honest dead ends rather than judgmental error messages.
projects.author: Faculty Contributor (Pending)
projects.purpose: Mechanism reasoning is the difference between memorizing reactions and actually doing organic chemistry. Most students get the reps too late.
projects.ai_role: Claude generated the SVG arrow animations from mechanism templates supplied by the contributor. The faculty member rewrote several intermediate structures that were drawn correctly but stylistically inconsistent with the textbook conventions students were learning.
projects.human_review: Cross-checked against current organic chemistry textbooks; every mechanism has a citation; arrow conventions deliberately match course materials.
projects.accessibility_notes: Step-by-step text equivalent of every animation, keyboard-controlled stepping, contrast tested for color-blind users.

projects.title: Titration Prediction Tool
projects.description: A pre-lab prediction tool. Students propose what their titration curve will look like before they run the experiment; the app overlays the predicted curve with the theoretical curve and flags where their reasoning diverged. Used the day before lab, not as a substitute for it.
projects.author: Faculty Contributor (Pending)
projects.purpose: Pre-lab work usually means reading. This app makes pre-lab work mean predicting, which produces better lab attention.
projects.ai_role: Claude generated the prediction-comparison logic from contributor-supplied curve data. The faculty member rewrote the divergence-flagging language so it described the chemistry rather than scoring the student.
projects.human_review: Reviewed by chemistry faculty; predicted-curve fidelity verified across the standard CHEM 220 titration set.
projects.accessibility_notes: Drag-and-curve input with keyboard alternative, full numerical entry mode, plain-language divergence explanations.

projects.title: Physics Motion Lab
projects.description: Students manipulate position, velocity, acceleration, friction, and force on a virtual sandbox and watch motion unfold in real time. The reflection prompts ask students to make a prediction first and to explain afterward — the trace remains on screen so they can compare their mental model to the simulation.
projects.author: Faculty Contributor (Pending)
projects.purpose: Newtonian intuition is built by being wrong predictably. This app supplies the predictable wrongness.
projects.ai_role: Claude generated the simulation physics and prompt set. The contributor verified all simulation behavior at edge cases (zero friction, high acceleration) and rewrote prompts that pushed students toward textbook explanations rather than physical reasoning.
projects.human_review: Validated against analytic solutions; reflection prompts pilot-tested across two PHYS 110 sections.
projects.accessibility_notes: Drag-and-drop with keyboard alternatives, sonification of motion option, color-blind-safe trace colors.

projects.title: Circuit Tinker Sandbox
projects.description: A drag-and-connect circuit builder. Students wire batteries, resistors, capacitors, and switches into series and parallel arrangements; the app calculates current, voltage, and power instantly and surfaces a brief plain-language note when the student's circuit reveals a common conceptual issue (such as treating series-parallel boundaries inconsistently).
projects.author: Faculty Contributor (Pending)
projects.purpose: Most circuit struggles are conceptual, not computational. This app catches the concept before the calculation goes wrong.
projects.ai_role: Claude generated the circuit-evaluation engine and the misconception-detection logic. The contributor verified the evaluation engine against textbook circuits and rewrote misconception messages that read as schoolmasterly.
projects.human_review: Reviewed by physics faculty; misconception bank is tagged by source so other instructors can extend.
projects.accessibility_notes: Drag-and-drop with keyboard alternatives, full numerical entry mode, screen-reader-friendly circuit description view.

projects.title: Wave Interference Explorer
projects.description: An interference visualizer where students adjust source positions, frequencies, and phases and watch the resulting pattern shift. The app pairs each visual with the math that produced it, so students can see what the equations are actually saying.
projects.author: Faculty Contributor (Pending)
projects.purpose: Wave equations describe what students could already feel if they had a way to feel it. This app is the way.
projects.ai_role: Claude generated the interference rendering and the math-to-visual pairing. The contributor verified phase behavior at edge cases and rewrote pairing captions that mistook visual symmetry for mathematical symmetry.
projects.human_review: Reviewed by physics faculty; rendering accuracy validated against analytic solutions.
projects.accessibility_notes: Color-blind-safe palette, full numerical readout alongside visualization, motion-sensitivity warning and pause control.

projects.title: Statistics Sampling Calculator
projects.description: A sampling-error sandbox. Students change population parameters, sample size, and confidence level; the app draws repeated samples and shows the resulting distribution of estimates. Confidence intervals stop being a formula and start being a thing students can watch.
projects.author: Faculty Contributor (Pending)
projects.purpose: The intuition that 'the sample is not the population' is built by drawing a hundred samples and watching them disagree.
projects.ai_role: Claude generated the sampling visualization and explanatory text. The contributor rewrote captions that used 'probability that the true mean is in the interval' phrasing and replaced it with frequentist-correct language.
projects.human_review: Reviewed by mathematics faculty; statistical interpretations checked twice for common phrasings that confuse confidence with probability.
projects.accessibility_notes: Color-blind-safe distribution charts, full numerical tables alongside visualization, plain-language interpretation panels.

projects.title: Calculus Tangent Tracer
projects.description: Students draw or select a function and drag a point along its curve; the app shows the tangent line at that point alongside the derivative function being built up in real time. The derivative emerges as a graph rather than as a formula to memorize.
projects.author: Faculty Contributor (Pending)
projects.purpose: Show students that the derivative is itself a function before introducing the rules for computing it.
projects.ai_role: Claude generated the curve-rendering and derivative-tracing logic. The contributor verified behavior at non-differentiable points (corners, cusps, vertical tangents) and rewrote the explanatory captions for those cases.
projects.human_review: Reviewed by mathematics faculty; derivative behavior validated against analytic forms.
projects.accessibility_notes: Drag-and-drop with keyboard arrow alternative, full numerical readout, screen-reader-friendly description of the tangent direction.

projects.title: Proof Structure Mapper
projects.description: Students paste their draft proof; the app reflects back the logical structure — what's been assumed, what's been claimed, where the argument turns — and asks the student to mark each step as 'this follows from definition,' 'this uses a previous result,' or 'this is where I'm stuck.' The map is the diagnostic.
projects.author: Faculty Contributor (Pending)
projects.purpose: Most failed proofs are organizationally failed, not logically failed. This app shows the organization before judging the logic.
projects.ai_role: Claude generated the structural extraction logic from a faculty-supplied set of proof patterns. The contributor flagged places where the AI imposed structure rather than reflecting it and rebuilt those segments.
projects.human_review: Reviewed by mathematics faculty; the tool deliberately does not certify proofs as correct — that's still the human's job.
projects.accessibility_notes: Text-only input, structure shown as both diagram and outline, exportable as a study aid.

projects.title: Qualitative Theme Weaver
projects.description: A coding environment for interview excerpts and field notes. Students propose codes, attach excerpts, and watch the codebook shape itself; the app surfaces patterns and asks the researcher to confirm or revise — without proposing themes that the data hasn't earned.
projects.author: Faculty Contributor (Pending)
projects.purpose: Qualitative coding is interpretive work. The tool's job is to make the work visible, not to do it.
projects.ai_role: Claude generated the pattern-detection logic. The contributor was firm that the AI must not propose themes; the app only echoes patterns the researcher has already coded toward, which preserves the interpretive labor as the student's own.
projects.human_review: Reviewed by qualitative methods faculty; output explicitly frames itself as a working draft, not a finding.
projects.accessibility_notes: Side-by-side excerpt-and-code view, full keyboard navigation, exportable codebook in plain text.

projects.title: Social Survey Design Bench
projects.description: Students draft survey questions and walk through a checklist that surfaces double-barreled questions, leading framings, response-set bias, and response-option problems. The app does not rewrite the question; it reflects the issue and lets the student rewrite, and the version history shows how question quality improved across iterations.
projects.author: Faculty Contributor (Pending)
projects.purpose: The fastest way to learn what makes a good survey question is to write a bad one and see why it fails.
projects.ai_role: Claude generated the diagnostic checklist from a methodology source bank supplied by the contributor. The faculty member rejected several diagnostics that flagged adequate questions as flawed and tightened those rules.
projects.human_review: Reviewed by research methods faculty; checklist sources cited and dated.
projects.accessibility_notes: Single-question-at-a-time mode, plain-language diagnostic descriptions, exportable revision history.

projects.title: Stratification Budget Walker
projects.description: Students try to make a household budget work across several income tiers using realistic local cost data — rent, food, transportation, healthcare, childcare. The app does not lecture; it simply makes visible which decisions become easier and which become impossible at each tier.
projects.author: Faculty Contributor (Pending)
projects.purpose: Stratification is taught with statistics; this app teaches it with constraints. The lesson is in what the budget can't absorb.
projects.ai_role: Claude generated the cost data structure and decision logic from regional cost-of-living sources supplied by the contributor. The faculty member rewrote several scenarios that read as poverty tourism and added scenarios with multiple plausible right answers.
projects.human_review: Reviewed by sociology faculty; cost data is sourced, dated, and editable; scenarios deliberately avoid assuming a single 'correct' choice.
projects.accessibility_notes: Plain-language scenarios, no scoring, content-warning landing page, exit-anytime button.

projects.title: Cognitive Bias Mirror
projects.description: A series of short scenarios that elicit common cognitive biases — anchoring, availability, confirmation, framing. Students make a quick judgment, then see the bias their answer pattern suggests; the app shows how the same question can be reframed to elicit a different answer from the same person.
projects.author: Faculty Contributor (Pending)
projects.purpose: Reading about biases doesn't reduce them. Watching yourself fall into one might.
projects.ai_role: Claude generated the scenario set from cognitive-psychology source material supplied by the contributor. The faculty member rewrote several scenarios that produced the bias too obviously and added pairs of reframings that landed students in different answers.
projects.human_review: Reviewed by psychology faculty; scenarios cite their source experiments and avoid presenting bias as a deficit.
projects.accessibility_notes: Text-only, untimed, no scoring of individual responses, exportable summary of bias patterns observed.

projects.title: Therapeutic Listening Rehearsal
projects.description: Text-based practice for the foundational skills of therapeutic conversation: open questions, reflective listening, attending to silence. The app provides a client voice with a complaint; students respond, and the app surfaces what their response did rather than scoring it.
projects.author: Faculty Contributor (Pending)
projects.purpose: Counseling skills can't be tested on a quiz. This app provides reps in a low-stakes environment before students sit with real clients.
projects.ai_role: Claude drafted client voices from clinical training material. The contributor (a counseling faculty member) rewrote dialog that flattened presenting concerns into types and removed two scenarios that read as voyeuristic.
projects.human_review: Reviewed by counseling faculty; scenarios labeled by skill focus; no scoring, only reflection.
projects.accessibility_notes: Text-only, content-warning landing page, exit-anytime button, pause-between-turns by default.

projects.title: Memory Mechanics Tester
projects.description: Replicates classic memory experiments — serial position, depth of processing, encoding specificity — with the student as participant. Students see their own data plotted against published norms and write a brief reflection on where their results converged and diverged with the classic finding.
projects.author: Faculty Contributor (Pending)
projects.purpose: Reading about memory experiments is one thing. Being the participant in your own version is another.
projects.ai_role: Claude generated the experiment scaffolding and classic-norms data set. The contributor verified that experimental procedures matched original methodologies closely enough for valid comparison and rewrote several reflection prompts that asked students to over-claim from a single trial.
projects.human_review: Reviewed by psychology faculty; sources cited; the app is explicit that one student's run is not a replication.
projects.accessibility_notes: Plain-language instructions, untimed practice mode, optional auditory presentation, full transcript of stimuli.

projects.title: Primary Source Cross-Examiner
projects.description: Students upload or paste a primary source and walk through a structured cross-examination: who, when, why, for whom, what's missing, what's at stake. The app reflects the answers back as a source-criticism brief — what this source can support, what it can't, and what corroboration would strengthen the claims.
projects.author: Faculty Contributor (Pending)
projects.purpose: Source criticism is taught implicitly through reading lists; this app makes the reasoning explicit so students can practice it deliberately.
projects.ai_role: Claude generated the question scaffolding from a faculty-supplied historical-methods rubric. The contributor rewrote several prompts that anachronistically projected modern source-evaluation criteria onto pre-modern documents.
projects.human_review: Reviewed by history faculty; the framework is deliberately period-flexible rather than period-neutral.
projects.accessibility_notes: Text-only, plain-language prompts, exportable as a source-criticism brief, screen-reader-tested.

projects.title: Oral History Interview Rehearsal
projects.description: Students rehearse oral history interviews with AI narrators based on archival profiles. The app does not impersonate real historical figures; the narrators are composites whose responses model the kinds of memory dynamics — gaps, reconstruction, emotional weight — that students will encounter with real narrators.
projects.author: Faculty Contributor (Pending)
projects.purpose: Oral history training tends to focus on the consent process and the recording equipment. This app focuses on what to do when the narrator's memory disagrees with the archive.
projects.ai_role: Claude generated narrator voices from composite profiles supplied by the contributor. The faculty member was firm that no real person be impersonated and rewrote several narrator responses that read as too narratively tidy.
projects.human_review: Reviewed by oral history faculty; the synthetic-narrator framing is repeated explicitly throughout the app.
projects.accessibility_notes: Text-only, transcripts available throughout, content-warning landing page, exit-anytime button.

projects.title: Contextual Timeline Builder
projects.description: Students drag events onto a multi-strand timeline — political, cultural, intellectual, economic — and the app surfaces what was happening contemporaneously across strands when their event occurred. The point is to break the habit of seeing history as a single thread.
projects.author: Faculty Contributor (Pending)
projects.purpose: Most history surveys teach one strand at a time. This app makes the simultaneity visible.
projects.ai_role: Claude generated the contemporaneous-event suggestions from period sources supplied by the contributor. The faculty member rewrote several entries that were geographically narrow (Eurocentric default) and added entries from regions and traditions the AI initially under-represented.
projects.human_review: Reviewed by history faculty; sources cited per event; the dataset is editable.
projects.accessibility_notes: Drag-and-drop with keyboard alternatives, color-blind-safe strand palette, full timeline data table.

projects.title: Seminar Question Forge
projects.description: Students paste a reading; the app asks structured prompts about the text and generates open-ended seminar questions across registers — clarifying, comparative, applied, dissenting. Each question links back to the passage that prompted it so the seminar can return to the text rather than orbit it.
projects.author: Faculty Contributor (Pending)
projects.purpose: Good seminar questions are taught implicitly. This app makes the structure of question-making visible enough to practice.
projects.ai_role: Claude generated question templates from a discussion-pedagogy bank supplied by the contributor. The faculty member rewrote questions that read as quiz prompts dressed up as discussion and tightened the link between question and passage so students couldn't bluff.
projects.human_review: Reviewed by philosophy faculty; output is explicitly a draft for the discussion leader to edit, not a finished question set.
projects.accessibility_notes: Text-only input, plain-language question categories, exportable seminar plan document.

projects.title: Ethics Scenario Decision Lab
projects.description: Walks students through ambiguous ethical scenarios in classroom, research, and professional contexts. After each decision, the app surfaces which ethical frameworks support, complicate, or contradict the choice — without crowning a winner. The friction between frameworks is the lesson.
projects.author: Faculty Contributor (Pending)
projects.purpose: Ethics teaching often presents frameworks as menu items. This app shows them as overlapping commitments that pull in different directions on real cases.
projects.ai_role: Claude drafted scenarios and framework reflections from contributor-supplied ethics literature. The faculty member rewrote several scenarios that telegraphed the 'right' answer and added cases where the frameworks genuinely diverge.
projects.human_review: Reviewed by philosophy faculty; the tool deliberately does not score answers; framework summaries cite their sources.
projects.accessibility_notes: Plain-language framework summaries, no time pressure, content-warning landing page where applicable.

projects.title: Logic Fallacy Trainer
projects.description: A practice space for spotting and naming informal fallacies in real-world arguments — political ads, op-eds, social media, advertising. Students identify the move, name the fallacy, and explain what would actually be needed to support the claim.
projects.author: Faculty Contributor (Pending)
projects.purpose: Naming fallacies is necessary but not sufficient. The app pushes students to articulate what good evidence would look like, not just what the bad version was.
projects.ai_role: Claude generated example arguments from a faculty-supplied case bank. The contributor rewrote examples that targeted any one political position and replaced them with a balanced set across the political spectrum.
projects.human_review: Reviewed by philosophy faculty; examples are sourced, dated, and labeled across the political spectrum to avoid partisan instruction.
projects.accessibility_notes: Text-only, no time pressure, exportable practice journal.

projects.title: Composition Grid Tester
projects.description: Students upload an image and the app overlays compositional grids — rule of thirds, golden ratio, dynamic symmetry, diagonal lines — letting them toggle and compare. The point is not to verify that the image is 'right' but to surface what the artist's eye was already responding to.
projects.author: Faculty Contributor (Pending)
projects.purpose: Compositional theory becomes meaningful when students see it in their own work, not in canonical examples.
projects.ai_role: Claude generated the grid-overlay logic. The contributor (a studio art faculty member) rejected the AI's initial scoring panel; the version that ships only displays the grids and lets students draw conclusions for themselves.
projects.human_review: Reviewed by studio faculty; the deliberate absence of scoring is documented as a pedagogical choice.
projects.accessibility_notes: Image uploads stay local in the browser, alt-text prompts for accessibility, color-blind-safe overlay colors.

projects.title: Type Pairing Bench
projects.description: Students try font pairings on sample layouts — body, display, accent — with sliders for weight, size, and tracking. The app shows what each pairing communicates rather than whether it's 'right,' and offers similar pairings as starting points when the student gets stuck.
projects.author: Faculty Contributor (Pending)
projects.purpose: Typography is taught with rules, but practiced with a feel. This app builds the feel through structured experimentation.
projects.ai_role: Claude generated the pairing-similarity database from a curated set of canonical pairings supplied by the contributor. The faculty member rewrote the communication-tone descriptions to remove generic 'modern' and 'classic' tags and added more specific rhetorical descriptors.
projects.human_review: Reviewed by design faculty; pairing data is editable so other instructors can extend.
projects.accessibility_notes: Sample text editable to user-supplied content, descriptive labels for every pairing, screen-reader-friendly type comparisons.

projects.title: Interview Rehearsal Room
projects.description: Students conduct text-based practice interviews with AI subjects across registers — the reluctant source, the over-eager source, the expert who keeps drifting off-topic. The app reflects on the questions students asked: what they elicited, what they shut down, where the silence went.
projects.author: Faculty Contributor (Pending)
projects.purpose: Interview reps are hard to come by in coursework. This app supplies low-stakes ones with the kinds of subjects students will eventually meet.
projects.ai_role: Claude played subjects from interest profiles supplied by the contributor. The faculty member tightened the profiles after early tests showed subjects were too cooperative and added an off-topic-drift behavior the AI was not naturally producing.
projects.human_review: Reviewed by journalism faculty; subject behaviors are inspectable so students can debrief honestly.
projects.accessibility_notes: Text-only, transcripts always available, pause-and-resume support, exit-anytime button.

projects.title: Speech Pacing Mirror
projects.description: Students paste their speech text and walk through a pacing analysis — sentence length variability, transition density, breath points, places where structure is doing the work and places where it isn't. The app does not score the speech; it makes the rhythm visible so the student can revise with awareness.
projects.author: Faculty Contributor (Pending)
projects.purpose: Most speech anxiety is about delivery, but most speech weakness is about structure. This app surfaces structure before the student worries about delivery.
projects.ai_role: Claude generated the pacing-analysis logic. The contributor rewrote several output panels that read as judgmental and reframed them as observational.
projects.human_review: Reviewed by communication faculty; output is positioned as a revision tool, not a scoring tool.
projects.accessibility_notes: Text-only input, screen-reader-tested rhythm visualization, exportable revision worksheet.
